steps.text: Determine if the facility radiation protection program has been maintained in accordance with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments, since the last inspection.
Determine if the licensee’s procedures for radiation protection at the facility meet regulatory requirements, and licensee commitments.
Determine if Form NRC-3, 'Notice to Employees,' is posted as required.
Determine if restricted areas are posted and controlled as required.
Determine if appropriate protective clothing is used by facility personnel as required.
Determine if the licensee’s personnel dosimetry program is effective, maintained and meets regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine if licensee personnel, equipment and materials exiting a controlled area are monitored as required.
Determine if personnel working with or around radioactive materials have been instructed in radiation protection as required.
Determine if required radiation surveys, sampling and monitoring were performed in accordance with regulatory requirements and licensee procedures.
Determine if the licensee has implemented a calibration program for the survey, sampling and monitoring instruments in use as required.
Determine if exposure limits in 10 CFR Part 20 were satisfied.
Determine if changes with the potential to affect radiation protection activities were consistent with 10 CFR 50.59, the license and Technical Specification (TS) requirements.
Determine if the licensee addressed radiologically significant events in accordance with regulatory and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine if an ALARA program has been implemented as required.
Determine if the licensee has developed, documented and implemented a radiation protection program in accordance with regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1101).
Determine if the respiratory protection program meets regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1701-4) and licensee commitments.
Determine if planned special exposures were made in accordance with regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1206) and licensee procedures.
Determine if the dose to the embryo/fetus for declared pregnant women met regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1208).
Observe the protective clothing worn by two facility experimenters or other respective personnel during their work activities.
Discuss with two individuals to determine their use of protective clothing.
Check the licensee’s procedures or precautions posted at the entrance to controlled areas for requirements related to protective clothing.
Ensure personnel are instructed in the proper use and function of the protective clothing in accordance with 10 CFR 19.12.
Verify the provisions for the correct disposition of contaminated protective clothing.
Observe the personnel dosimetry worn by four facility workers, including one operator and one experimenter.
Check if the dosimetry devices issued to facility personnel were appropriate for the type and energy of the emitted radiation.
Instruct facility personnel who are required to use dosimeters in the proper use and function of the dosimeters.
Use dosimeters.
Check alarming dosimeters (chirpers) before each operation to assure that they are operational.
Assess the need for extremity monitors for handling activated samples and contaminated experimental equipment.
Observe two selected facility workers leaving the applicable controlled area to determine if the dosimeter readings are actually recorded as required.
Review the dosimeter processing process, whether done by the licensee or commercially.
Conduct an exit survey.
Check all personnel, tools, equipment, and protective clothing for contamination before leaving a controlled area.
Use handheld friskers, stand-in monitors, portal monitors, or hand and foot monitors for exit monitoring.
Shield the exit monitor from extraneous radiation sources.
Monitor hands before removing the probe from its mounting when using handheld friskers.
Use a hand-held gas flow proportional counter to detect alpha contamination if suspected.
Conduct smear survey and analyses using a gas flow proportional counter if alpha contamination is suspected.
Perform nasal smears where personnel contamination by alpha emitters is suspected.
Evaluate the radiation safety training received by two permanent facility workers.
Observe respective activities and discuss the radiation safety training received by these individuals.
Confirm that appropriate training was actually received and understood.
Assess if the instruction appropriate to the tasks was provided and if there was adequate supervision.
Conduct observations of two radiological surveys, sampling or monitoring activities.
Accompany a person qualified in the licensee’s radiation control techniques on normal rounds.
Request to be shown the manner in which radiation surveys, sampling, or monitoring tasks are normally accomplished.
Perform operability checks on all instrumentation before use, e.g., source and battery checks.
Examine the licensee’s training records to confirm that technicians are sufficiently trained.
Determine whether personnel conducting radiation surveys are qualified by training and experience.
Examine surveys of solid waste material to determine whether the licensee complies with their procedures and requirements.
Verify the calibration of radiation monitoring instrument(s) used in the licensee’s radiation surveys.

preconditions.text: If volatile radioactive materials are used in an area, it is required to be posted and controlled for airborne contamination.
Do not enter a high radiation area without a licensee escort trained in radiation protection who is knowledgeable of the high radiation area and the health physics precautions to be taken.
If the potential exists for airborne contamination, ensure levels are within the regulatory requirements of 10 CFR Part 20, Subparts C and D.

constraints.text: Radiologically controlled areas are required to be conspicuously posted.
High radiation areas are required to be strictly controlled to prevent unauthorized or inadvertent access.
Protective clothing may include gloves, booties, rubber boots, hair covers, lab coats, coveralls, and rubber or plastic aprons for use with liquids.
Some articles of protective clothing may need to be disposed of as radioactive waste.
It is important that dosimetry is worn as required.
Dosimeter processing should meet the requirements of 10 CFR 20.1501 for accreditation.
